industrial-ontario-ca.summary: The Ontario, CA industrial submarket (Inland Empire West) is in a post-peak recalibration phase. Direct vacancy has climbed to roughly 7.7% to 8.5% in early 2026, up sharply from sub-2% pandemic-era lows, as a wave of speculative construction delivered vacant space faster than demand could absorb it. Lease rates have fallen roughly 34% from their 2023 peak to around $0.99 to $1.08 per SF per month NNN, giving tenants meaningful negotiating leverage including free-rent concessions. Sale prices have pulled back to roughly $220 to $320 per SF depending on asset quality and size, with cap rates averaging in the mid-4% to low-5% range for stabilized product.
industrial-ontario-ca.market_trend: Industrial sale prices in Ontario/Inland Empire West pulled back roughly 15% from 2022 peak levels through mid-2026, with the average transaction price settling in the $259-$275/SF range during 2025, compared to highs above $300/SF in 2022.
value_drivers: Rising vacancy (7.7%-8.5% direct in Q1 2026) driven by excess speculative deliveries is pushing lease rates and values down
Lease rates down roughly 34% from 2023 peak to ~$0.99-$1.08 NNN/SF/month, eroding NOI and limiting upward pricing pressure on sales
Scarcity of smaller sub-100,000 SF modern logistics product is keeping that size band more competitive than big-box
Proximity to Ontario International Airport and I-10/I-15/SR-60 interchange continues to support long-term logistics demand and prevents deeper value declines

industrial-dallas-tx.summary: The Dallas industrial market is one of the most active in the United States, with vacancy declining to 8.7-8.8% in Q1 2026 and average asking rents reaching approximately $10.24/SF NNN annually. Investment sale pricing averaged $143-147/SF in Q1 2026, with cap rates holding near 6.2% on stabilized assets. Demand from 3PL, e-commerce, and logistics users is outpacing new deliveries for the first time in several years, signaling a tightening leasing environment ahead, though elevated sublease availability continues to give tenants some negotiating leverage.
industrial-dallas-tx.market_trend: Dallas-Fort Worth industrial sale prices trended upward from an average of approximately $129/SF in Q4 2025 to $143-147/SF by Q1 2026, reflecting continued investor demand for stabilized logistics assets despite a more cautious transaction environment.
value_drivers: Vacancy declining 137 bps from peak to 8.7-8.8%, reducing tenant leverage in bulk logistics submarkets
Elevated sublease availability near 10.5% of market continues to suppress effective rents and compress pricing on value-add assets
Infill submarkets (South Stemmons, DFW Airport corridor) face land scarcity, pushing premium pricing well above metro averages
Record 2025 leasing volume of 56 million SF - 50% above long-term norms - driven by 3PL and e-commerce users supporting rent growth

industrial-atlanta-ga.summary: Atlanta's metro industrial market is in a gradual recovery after vacancy peaked near 9% in mid-2025. Vacancy has since declined to approximately 7.5-7.6% as of Q1 2026, driven by improved leasing absorption and a sharp pullback in new construction deliveries. Average sale prices for metro warehouses have ranged from roughly $90 to $179 per square foot according to CBRE and Colliers, with the all-market average hitting an all-time high of approximately $146 per square foot in Q4 2025. NNN asking rents have softened to around $8.81 per square foot annually but are expected to firm as the pipeline tightens further.
industrial-atlanta-ga.market_trend: Average industrial sale prices in metro Atlanta rose sharply to an all-time high of approximately $146 per square foot in Q4 2025, up 34.5% quarter-over-quarter, after ranging between $90 and $179 per square foot across 2024-2025 transactions per CBRE and Colliers.
value_drivers: Vacancy compression from 9.0% in mid-2025 to 7.5-7.6% by Q1 2026 is tightening available supply and supporting pricing
New construction deliveries have fallen sharply to an 11-year low in Q4 2025, constraining future supply and limiting rent-dilutive new product
Large-block mid-size space (100,000-500,000 SF) faces highest availability, making smaller infill assets relatively scarce and better priced
Tariff-related supply chain reshoring activity and sustained e-commerce demand from I-85 North and I-20 West submarkets are driving leasing velocity

industrial-phoenix-az.summary: The Phoenix metro industrial market is actively recovering after absorbing over 91 million SF of new supply delivered between 2023 and 2025. Vacancy has been declining for four consecutive quarters, reaching roughly 9.2% to 12.4% depending on the source and methodology, as Q1 2026 absorption of 4.8 to 5.0 million SF outpaced new deliveries of just 1.4 million SF by a 3-to-1 margin. Sale prices rose sharply in Q1 2026 to an average of $245 per SF, up 17% year-over-year, with the largest deals concentrated in Class A logistics buildings in the Northwest and Southwest corridors. Small-bay and infill industrial space remains the tightest segment of the market, with vacancy as low as 4 to 6%, while large-format logistics buildings face more competition from available sublease space.
industrial-phoenix-az.market_trend: Phoenix industrial sale prices rose approximately 17% year-over-year to an average of $245 per SF in Q1 2026, recovering from a 2024-2025 trough driven by record oversupply, with institutional capital re-engaging aggressively for stabilized Class A assets.
value_drivers: Vacancy compression: four consecutive quarters of declining vacancy as absorption outpaces sharply reduced new deliveries (down 82% YOY in Q1 2026)
Semiconductor and advanced manufacturing demand surge: TSMC, Amkor, and 60+ industry expansions since 2020 driving structural industrial demand independent of logistics cycle
Sharp bifurcation between small-bay (4-6% vacancy, rent growth) and large-format logistics (16%+ vacancy, oversupply still working through)
Tariff-driven metals inflation (steel up 20.7% YOY) and power/electrical gear lead times of 42-52 weeks constraining new construction starts and supporting existing asset values

industrial-columbus-oh.summary: The Columbus, OH industrial market has shown strong momentum over the past two years, with industrial vacancy falling sharply from 9.4% in Q1 2025 to 6.6% by Q1 2026 as demand significantly outpaced new supply. Investment sales volume reached $1.1 billion through the first nine months of 2025, the highest since 2022, driven by both institutional and private buyers. Average asking NNN lease rates hover around $9 to $10 per square foot, while sale prices across the market average roughly $95 to $155 per square foot depending on building class and submarket. Modern Class A bulk distribution facilities and Rickenbacker logistics corridor assets command significant premiums over general industrial product.
industrial-columbus-oh.market_trend: Industrial sale prices in Columbus have firmed over the 2024-2026 window, with total investment volume reaching $1.1 billion through Q3 2025 (highest since 2022) and average per-square-foot pricing supported by institutional re-entry and record demand absorption.
value_drivers: Vacancy falling sharply from 9.4% in Q1 2025 to 6.6% in Q1 2026 as net absorption far exceeded new deliveries
Speculative construction pipeline at its smallest since 2019 with deliveries slowing 39% year-over-year, tightening available supply
Scarcity of modern Class A bulk distribution product, which commands rents 9% above general industrial and drives outsized sale prices along Rickenbacker logistics corridor
Growing manufacturing demand from Anduril Arsenal-1 and onshoring activity increasing competition for mid-size and flex industrial space

office-dallas-tx.summary: The Dallas office market is highly bifurcated: trophy and Class A Uptown and Preston Center assets attracted a surge of institutional capital in 2024-2026, with sale prices ranging from roughly $350/SF to over $747/SF for the best buildings, while overall metro vacancy remains elevated near 24-25% and Class B properties continue to post negative absorption. Asking rents hit a record high of $33-$34/SF full-service across the metro in early 2026, with Uptown commanding roughly $60/SF. The strongest leasing and investment activity is concentrated in Uptown, Preston Center, and the North Dallas Corridor, while the CBD and Las Colinas submarkets face ongoing headwinds from high vacancy and tenant consolidations.
office-dallas-tx.market_trend: Sale prices for Dallas office assets rose sharply for institutional-quality properties in 2025, with the two largest Uptown trades pricing at $639-$747/SF and total metro sales volume jumping from $107M in all of 2024 to $1.46B through August 2025, though distressed and Class B assets continued to trade at steep discounts or go to foreclosure.
value_drivers: Trophy and Class A office vacancy in Uptown and Preston Center tightened sharply, with Preston Center availability at just 6.8% in H1 2026, creating significant rent and pricing premiums over the metro average
Class B office posted 493,000 SF of negative net absorption in Q1 2026, widening the pricing gap between quality tiers and pushing commodity office cap rates above 7.5% to clear
No new office deliveries in Q1 2026 for the first time in 30 years, reducing supply-side pressure in submarkets with strong demand and supporting rent growth of 6% year-over-year
AT&T's planned exit of 2 million SF from Downtown Dallas by 2028 is expected to push CBD vacancy well above its current 33%, depressing values in that submarket

office-atlanta-ga.summary: The Atlanta office market remains a tale of two cities: premier Class A and Trophy buildings in Midtown and Buckhead are trading at $158 to $228 per square foot with strong leasing demand driven by a flight-to-quality trend, while the broader market averaged $131 per square foot in 2025, down slightly from 2024. Overall vacancy hovers near 21 to 27 percent depending on the source, but the construction pipeline is at its lowest level in over a decade, which is expected to tighten supply and support Class A rents going forward. Investment sales activity in 2025 surged 56 percent year-over-year, signaling returning buyer confidence, though many assets are still trading well below replacement cost. Tenants are consolidating into higher-quality spaces, leaving older Class B and C product with elevated vacancy and flat rental rates.
office-atlanta-ga.market_trend: Office sale prices in Atlanta declined modestly in 2025, with the average price per square foot falling 2.66% year-over-year to $131/SF broadly, though top-tier Midtown Class A assets traded at $158 to $228/SF, outperforming the market average as investors selectively targeted stabilized, well-leased buildings.
value_drivers: Flight-to-quality: 73.5% of new leasing activity concentrated in Trophy/Class A assets, leaving Class B/C with elevated vacancy and flat absorption
Supply scarcity: construction pipeline at lowest level since 2011, with only ~272,000 SF under construction metro-wide, reducing future supply pressure
Persistent high vacancy: overall market vacancy of 26-27% is keeping broad sale prices well below 2019 peaks and replacement cost
Return-to-office mandates: corporate RTO policies are supporting leasing demand in premium Midtown and Buckhead submarkets, pushing Class A asking rents above $40/SF

retail-phoenix-az.summary: The Phoenix retail market is performing strongly entering mid-2026, with vacancy holding near 5.0% and average asking rents reaching $26.57/SF in Q4 2025 after five-plus consecutive years of annual rent growth averaging around 5-6%. Investment activity has accelerated, with transaction volume in 2025 running more than 25% above 2024 levels and new-construction grocery-anchored centers trading above $650/SF while larger power centers clear in the $250-$270/SF range. Unanchored and non-grocery-anchored strip centers typically trade at 7-8% cap rates, with top-tier stabilized assets compressing into the 6% range. No specific building address was provided for the target property, so a subject building size could not be determined from public records.
retail-phoenix-az.market_trend: Phoenix retail sale prices have risen significantly over the 2024-2026 window, with new-construction stabilized assets trading at record per-SF levels above $650/SF and the broader market averaging $187/SF on large-format investment-grade product, supported by accelerating transaction volume that surpassed $2 billion in the trailing twelve months through Q3 2025.
value_drivers: Sustained sub-5% retail vacancy driving landlord pricing power and above-market rent escalations
New-construction grocery-anchored and daily-needs centers commanding $600+/SF as private all-cash buyers bypass debt markets
Over $600M in maturing CMBS retail loans by 2026 may push value-add inventory to market, creating acquisition opportunities
National chain bankruptcies (99 Cents Only, Joann) temporarily lifted availability to 5.1% but space is being rapidly backfilled by off-price, fitness, and experiential tenants

industrial-fontana-ca.summary: The Fontana industrial submarket within Inland Empire West remains one of Southern California's most active logistics corridors, anchored by major freeway access to I-10, I-15, I-210, and SR-60. Institutional buyers including Bridge Logistics Properties and Rexford Industrial closed multiple large transactions between late 2024 and early 2026, with stabilized Class A assets trading in the $250 to $265 per square foot range. The market's vacancy rate stood at approximately 8.4 percent at year-end 2025, slightly elevated versus prior years due to a wave of new completions, yet demand from large logistics users including Amazon continues to absorb supply. Average asking NNN rents for warehouse and distribution space in Fontana are approximately $17 per square foot annually.
industrial-fontana-ca.market_trend: Stabilized mid-size Class A industrial sale prices in Fontana held in the $250-$265 per square foot range from late 2024 through early 2026, while large vacant big-box assets traded at a discount near $158 per square foot, reflecting bifurcated pricing between leased and vacant product.
value_drivers: Scarcity of large-format Class A industrial sites due to AB 98 and land constraints limiting new development pipelines
Elevated vacancy in the 8-9% range for big-box product above 500,000 SF creating pricing dislocation for mega-scale assets
Sustained demand from e-commerce and 3PL tenants such as Amazon absorbing mid-size and large distribution space
Proximity to I-10, I-15, and Ontario International Airport sustaining rent premiums for well-located Fontana product

industrial-las-vegas-nv.summary: The Las Vegas industrial market is working through the tail end of a supply-driven correction, with vacancy at roughly 11.4% in Q1 2026 but absorption trending positive at 5.1 million SF in 2025. Sale prices show a wide spread, from about $152/SF for large institutional big-box product up to $290/SF for smaller Class A infill assets, with the Southwest submarket commanding a clear premium. Asking rents have stabilized around $14 to $16 NNN annually, and most well-priced industrial product is clearing with little to no concession. The spec construction pipeline has effectively paused, which should support gradual absorption of standing vacancy through the rest of 2026.
industrial-las-vegas-nv.market_trend: Industrial sale prices in Las Vegas have bifurcated over the past 24 months: small-bay infill assets in the Southwest submarket have held or edged higher ($250-$300/SF range), while large-format distribution properties have seen price compression to $140-$165/SF as buyer discovery works through elevated vacancy.
value_drivers: Vacancy rose from sub-5% lows to 11.4% in Q1 2026 due to 2023-2025 supply wave, compressing values on large-bay product
Spec groundbreakings paused broadly across the market, reducing near-term pipeline and supporting rent stability
Sublease shadow supply reached 2.6 million SF, creating direct competition with landlords for mid-size tenants
Small-bay infill product under 25,000 SF in Southwest Las Vegas remains scarce and trades at a 60% premium to the broader valley

industrial-savannah-ga.summary: The Savannah industrial market remains one of the strongest in the Southeast, driven by record Port of Savannah cargo volumes and proximity to the Hyundai EV manufacturing plant. Vacancy rose through much of 2024-2025 due to a wave of speculative deliveries but began stabilizing near 9.9%-14.1% by early 2026 as new construction slowed. Average sale prices for Class A product tracked around $99-$137 per square foot in 2024-2025, while NNN asking rents for functional warehouse space in the immediate Savannah submarket ranged from roughly $8.54 to $12.00 per square foot annually. Investment activity remains robust with institutional and private buyers active across the Highway 21 corridor and port-adjacent submarkets.
industrial-savannah-ga.market_trend: Average industrial sale prices in Savannah rose from approximately $85-$100 per square foot in early 2024 to $130-$133 per square foot by Q4 2025, reflecting continued institutional demand despite rising vacancy from new supply deliveries.
value_drivers: Port of Savannah cargo volume growth creating sustained logistics tenant demand along I-16 and I-95 corridors
Hyundai EV plant generating 8,100 projected jobs and new supplier demand for proximate industrial space
Elevated vacancy (9.9%-14.1%) from 2023-2025 speculative construction wave pressuring rent growth and lease-up timelines
Declining new development pipeline in 2025-2026 beginning to rebalance supply and support stabilization

office-austin-tx.summary: Austin's office market remains tenant-favorable in mid-2026, with a citywide vacancy rate near 24-25% - one of the highest among major U.S. metros - giving tenants strong negotiating leverage on rents and concessions. Closed sale prices in 2025 averaged around $216/SF across all classes, above the national average of $192/SF, though individual deals ranged from under $130/SF for value-add suburban assets to over $400/SF for trophy downtown towers. Lease rates for Class A space run roughly $45-$65/SF full-service in the CBD and $28-$45/SF in suburban submarkets. A shrinking construction pipeline and early return-to-office mandates from major tech employers point toward gradual tightening of conditions through 2026 and into 2027.
office-austin-tx.market_trend: Austin office sale prices declined from a 2024 average of ~$403/SF (inflated by the $521.8M Block 185 trophy trade) to a 2025 average of ~$216/SF as institutional sellers like Starwood exited at deep discounts and value-add buyers dominated deal flow.
value_drivers: Elevated overall vacancy (~24.6% as of early 2026) suppressing values and keeping buyers cautious
Shrinking new construction pipeline (down 91% quarter-over-quarter in Q3 2025) reducing future supply pressure
Return-to-office mandates from tech tenants (NVIDIA 230K SF expansion, NinjaOne HQ) absorbing sublease inventory
Texas SB 840 enabling office-to-residential conversions creating optionality and floor for distressed asset pricing

office-nashville-tn.summary: Nashville's office market has held up better than most U.S. peers, with sale prices running near $205-226 per square foot and Class A asking rents averaging around $30-34 per square foot annually. Overall vacancy sits in the 17-19 percent range but is trending down as major corporate tenants like Oracle and Starbucks commit to large long-term leases. A slowing construction pipeline and strong tenant demand for amenity-rich space are the key positive forces heading into late 2026.
office-nashville-tn.market_trend: Nashville office sale prices rose from a 2024 average of approximately $124-205/SF to $217-226/SF by early 2026, reflecting continued investor appetite for stabilized Class A assets even as transaction volume remained thin with roughly 11 deals closed in all of 2024.
value_drivers: Flight-to-quality driving Class A lease-up while Class B/C vacancy widens, compressing values for older product
Construction pipeline near 5-year low of 1.8M SF, limiting new supply and supporting rents in premium buildings
Large corporate relocations (Oracle, Starbucks) absorbing significant blocks of space and validating trophy-asset pricing above $200/SF
Overall vacancy still elevated at 17-19 percent, keeping rent growth muted at roughly 1.0 percent annually and capping cap rate compression

office-charlotte-nc.summary: The Charlotte office market has seen a wave of deeply discounted sales over the past two years, with older Class A and B buildings in Uptown trading at $71 to $143 per square foot, often 40 to 55 percent below prior sale prices. Leasing momentum has sharply improved heading into 2026, with Q1 2026 recording one of the strongest quarters of leasing activity since the pandemic and trophy vacancy falling to 13.6 percent for buildings under ten years old. A clear two-tier market has emerged: newer and well-amenitized Class A assets command rents above $50 per square foot and are nearly fully leased, while older, less-occupied buildings remain under pricing pressure. Investors are acquiring distressed assets at reset basis and repositioning them, which signals early stabilization rather than full recovery.
office-charlotte-nc.market_trend: Office sale prices in Charlotte have trended downward for older assets over the 24-month window, with multiple Uptown towers transacting at $71 to $143 per square foot compared to prior sales of $200 or more per square foot, though distressed reset trades appear to be attracting new capital suggesting the market is approaching a floor.
value_drivers: Older Class A Uptown buildings selling at 40-55% discounts to prior trade prices, resetting basis for new buyers
Vacancy dipped to 16.6% in Q1 2026 - lowest since late 2023 - driven by large corporate lease signings (JP Morgan 137k SF, Charles Schwab 51.5k SF)
Scarcity of new Class A supply: only one major project under construction (Queensbridge Collective) and it is nearly fully pre-leased
Strong financial-services and professional-services demand concentrated in SouthPark and South End submarkets pushing Class A rents above $50/SF

office-tampa-fl.summary: Tampa's office market has remained active on a transaction basis, though volume sits well below the pre-pandemic five-year average of $1.1 billion annually. Sale prices across the market are highly fragmented by asset class and location, ranging from roughly $150/SF for distressed or high-vacancy Class B buildings to over $340/SF for fully occupied or specialty assets. Downtown Tampa and Westshore continue to outperform all other submarkets, with Class A rents exceeding $47/SF full-service and steady demand from institutional and healthcare users. Developers and hospital groups are the most active buyers, often acquiring traditional office buildings for repositioning or owner-use rather than pure investment.
office-tampa-fl.market_trend: Sale prices have largely plateaued around $184-$222/SF on average across the Tampa market since 2024, with the broadest pricing swings driven by asset class and occupancy rather than a clear directional price trend up or down.
value_drivers: Flight-to-quality compression: Class A/trophy availability rate in Downtown and Westshore fell to a three-year low of 16.8% in Q2 2026, pushing up rents and values for premium assets

Redevelopment premium: Developers and hospital groups are bidding above stabilized-investment values to acquire office sites for industrial, multifamily, or owner-use conversion, skewing sale prices upward
Buyer-seller price gap in Class B/C: Bids are coming in 5-15% below guidance on traditional office, suppressing transaction velocity and keeping reported sale prices variable
Limited new speculative supply: Only 303,000 SF under construction as of Q1 2026 and 53.5% preleased, limiting new competition and supporting existing building occupancy

office-denver-co.summary: Denver's office market is sharply bifurcated: downtown and CBD buildings continue to trade at historic discounts of 80-95% off peak values due to a 38% vacancy rate, while premium submarkets like Cherry Creek command top dollar with vacancy near 4.7%. Investors are buying distressed downtown towers at land-value prices, while well-located Class A product in sought-after neighborhoods still fetches $500-$925 per square foot. The overall market shows early signs of stabilization heading into 2026, driven by the completion of the 16th Street Mall renovation and growing return-to-office trends, but a full recovery in the CBD is expected to take years.
office-denver-co.market_trend: Denver office sale prices have diverged dramatically over the past 24 months: distressed CBD towers have sold at 75-95% discounts to prior peaks (some as low as $7/SF), while high-quality suburban and amenity-rich properties such as 255 Fillmore in Cherry Creek have transacted near or above $900/SF, making a single directional trend statement misleading for the market as a whole.
value_drivers: Record-high downtown CBD vacancy (~38% at end of 2025) pushing distressed Class B/C sale prices to land value or below
Cherry Creek and LoDo scarcity: Class A vacancy in Cherry Creek at 4.7%, the lowest on record, driving premium rents above $72/SF and sale prices above $900/SF
Sublease inventory falling 11% year-over-year, signaling gradual absorption and early recovery pressure on asking rents
$570M city bond program plus 16th Street Mall redevelopment completion injecting near-term optimism into downtown leasing fundamentals

office-san-diego-ca.summary: The San Diego office market has seen a wide range of sale pricing over the past 24 months, with suburban and amenity-rich campuses trading at premiums while downtown towers have changed hands at steep discounts to prior peak values. In 2025, 48 properties sold at an average of $267 per square foot, up sharply from $205 per square foot in 2024, driven by renewed investor appetite for well-located suburban and life science assets. Downtown vacancy remains above 35% and is the most distressed submarket, while UTC, Mission Valley, and Del Mar Heights are outperforming. Tenants and investors continue a strong flight to quality, favoring Class A, amenity-rich buildings and avoiding older or poorly located stock.
office-san-diego-ca.market_trend: San Diego office sale prices recovered meaningfully in 2025, with the average price per square foot rising from $205 in 2024 to $267 in 2025, though pricing remains highly bifurcated between discounted downtown towers selling at $155-$185/SF and premium suburban or life science assets trading at $300-$500/SF.
value_drivers: High downtown vacancy (35%+ per Yardi Matrix) continues to suppress CBD office values and widen bid-ask spreads
Flight-to-quality demand driving premium rents and values for Class A suburban assets in UTC, Mission Valley, and Del Mar Heights
Large speculative delivery pipeline (2.3M+ SF added 2024-2025) creating oversupply pressure particularly in downtown and life science submarkets
Mission Valley redevelopment catalyst (SDSU campus, Riverwalk, Civita totaling $10B+ in planned projects) supporting suburban submarket absorption

retail-orlando-fl.summary: Orlando's retail market remains very tight heading into mid-2026, with vacancy around 3.7% and average asking rents near $29.77 per square foot NNN, supported by continued population in-migration and tourism-driven consumer spending. Sale prices for retail assets have trended upward, with the market average hitting $301 per square foot in the 12 months through Q1 2025, up from $272 per square foot in Q1 2024. New supply is modest relative to demand, keeping landlords in a favorable position. Cap rates have compressed slightly, averaging around 6.3% across all retail subtypes, with NNN single-tenant credit deals trading tighter at 5.0 to 6.5%.
retail-orlando-fl.market_trend: Orlando retail sale prices rose approximately 10.7% per square foot from Q1 2024 ($272/SF avg) to Q1 2025 ($301/SF avg), reflecting continued investor demand and compressed cap rates in a low-vacancy market.
value_drivers: Sub-4% retail vacancy market-wide creating scarcity of available space and upward rent pressure
Sustained Florida population in-migration driving consumer spending and tenant demand
Tourism corridor on International Drive commanding premium pricing above market average
43 retail projects totaling 1.31M SF under construction with 82.5% pre-leased, limiting speculative oversupply risk

retail-las-vegas-nv.summary: The Las Vegas retail investment market is active and bifurcated: stabilized community and neighborhood centers are trading in the $238 to $340 per square foot range, while new-build or NNN single-tenant assets command sharp premiums driven by 1031 exchange demand and low available inventory. Market-wide vacancy held near 4.3% as of mid-2025 and NNN asking rents are approximately $22 per square foot annually, supporting continued investor interest despite a modest dip in taxable retail sales. Sales volume rebounded sharply in 2025, with 39 shopping-center transactions totaling over $408 million compared to just 20 in 2024.
retail-las-vegas-nv.market_trend: Las Vegas retail sale prices trended upward through the 24-month window, with H1 2025 shopping-center sales averaging $340/SF and anchored community centers like Peccole Plaza trading at $238/SF in April 2026, while the overall sales volume surge from 20 deals in 2024 to 39 deals in 2025 signals strengthening investor demand.
value_drivers: Severe scarcity of stabilized anchored centers for sale is driving aggressive pricing from 1031 exchange buyers
Vacancy compression to approximately 4.3% market-wide is pushing asking NNN rents upward toward $1.85/SF/month
Residential growth pipeline near Summerlin and North Las Vegas (Hylo Park, The Preserve at Badlands) is expanding the retail catchment and underpinning long-term tenant demand
Slowing taxable retail sales growth (-0.7% annually in Clark County) is creating caution in the value-add and non-essential retail segments

retail-san-antonio-tx.summary: San Antonio's retail market has remained tight through mid-2026, with vacancy holding near 4.2% and average sale prices ranging from $195 to $221 per square foot across the 2024-2026 window, well supported by steady tenant demand from fitness, discount, and grocery anchors. Average NNN asking rents stand at roughly $19.45/SF/yr as of Q1 2026, slightly off the 2025 peak but still near all-time highs. Cap rates have edged up from 6.1% in mid-2024 to 7.2% in Q1 2026, reflecting modest investor repricing amid higher-for-longer interest rates. The comps below are drawn from the San Antonio metro market because no specific submarket address was provided; they represent the broadest comparable set available from verified broker and news sources.
retail-san-antonio-tx.market_trend: Retail sale prices in San Antonio have softened modestly from an average of $248/SF in mid-2024 to approximately $211/SF in Q1 2026, a decline of roughly 15% over the 24-month window, as cap rate expansion outpaced NOI growth.
value_drivers: Near-record-low vacancy (4.2%) constraining tenant options and supporting landlord pricing power
Construction pipeline shrinking 18.5% annually, limiting new supply competition
Fitness, discount retail, and grocery anchor expansion absorbing large blocks of existing space
Rising cap rates (6.1% in mid-2024 to 7.2% in Q1 2026) creating slight downward pressure on investment sale prices

retail-sacramento-ca.summary: The Sacramento retail market is holding steady with vacancy around 5.8%-7.3% as of early 2026 and NNN asking rents averaging approximately $21-$25/SF/yr, supported by limited new construction and consistent tenant demand. Average retail sale prices softened to roughly $190-$261/SF in 2024-2025 per Kidder Mathews, reflecting elevated interest-rate pressure on investment values. Cap rates for stabilized retail in Sacramento average near 6.44%-6.65%, running 100-200 basis points above comparable Bay Area assets. The market is active for well-located strip centers and NNN assets, though individual deal volume has been constrained by the rate environment and bid-ask gaps.
retail-sacramento-ca.market_trend: Average retail sale prices in Sacramento declined approximately 8-9% year-over-year as of Q4 2025, falling from roughly $285/SF to approximately $261/SF per Kidder Mathews, reflecting buyer caution amid sustained elevated interest rates.
value_drivers: Retail vacancy edged up to 7.3% in Q1 2026 from 5.8% in Q3 2025, signaling mild softening that is pressuring asking prices
Construction pipeline contracted 14.7% year-over-year in Q4 2025, limiting new supply and supporting existing asking rents
Elevated financing rates (5.75%-6.5% as of late 2025) are widening bid-ask spreads and reducing individual deal volume in the investment market
1031 exchange demand from Bay Area sellers remains an active buyer pool for NNN-leased single-tenant retail, compressing cap rates on credit-tenanted assets

multifamily-atlanta-ga.summary: Atlanta's multifamily market is recovering from a 2022-2024 downturn driven by record new supply and rising borrowing costs. The median sale price reached roughly $156,000 to $189,500 per unit in 2025, with cap rates stabilizing in the low-to-mid 5% range. Investor appetite is returning, particularly for suburban and value-add assets, and deliveries are now falling sharply, which should support rent growth and tightening vacancy heading into 2026. Because no specific property address was provided, building square footage could not be determined from public records.
multifamily-atlanta-ga.market_trend: Atlanta multifamily sale prices dipped modestly in 2025, with the average price per unit falling from roughly $166,742 to $156,005 metro-wide as investors focused on value-add plays, though the median price for institutional-quality assets recovered to approximately $189,500 per unit by mid-2025, suggesting a two-tier market with premium assets holding value while workforce stock repriced.
value_drivers: Delivery pipeline falling more than 50% from its 2024 peak, reducing future supply pressure
Vacancy declined 200 basis points year-over-year through Q3 2025, tightening market conditions
Investor shift toward value-add and workforce assets nearly doubling volume in that segment in 2025
Strong in-migration supporting renter demand: metro Atlanta added ~75,100 residents between 2023 and 2024

multifamily-phoenix-az.summary: The Greater Phoenix multifamily market is in a late-cycle correction following a historic supply surge, but absorption has strengthened meaningfully in 2026. Average price per unit has declined to roughly $222,000 across the metro, though newer Class A properties in premier locations continue to trade at $290,000 to $340,000 per unit. Transaction volume is up year-over-year as investor confidence returns, led by institutional buyers targeting newly delivered and stabilized Class A assets, while Class B and C trades have also accelerated significantly.
multifamily-phoenix-az.market_trend: Multifamily sale prices in Greater Phoenix declined roughly 12% year-over-year on a per-unit basis through Q1 2026, though newly delivered Class A assets in supply-constrained submarkets bucked the trend and traded at firm or slightly improving valuations.
value_drivers: Record-high net absorption in Q1 2026 outpacing deliveries for first time since 2021, tightening effective vacancy
Construction pipeline down 30% year-over-year reducing future supply pressure and supporting price floors
Widespread concessions (6-8 weeks free rent) eroding Class A NOI and keeping going-in cap rates elevated
Investor broadening to Class B and C vintages driving 50-70% more trades in those categories versus 2024

multifamily-dallas-tx.summary: The Dallas multifamily market is working through an elevated supply cycle but is approaching equilibrium heading into late 2026. Transaction volume rose 3% in 2025 to over $4.3 billion, with the average price per unit recovering to roughly $168,000-$190,000 after bottoming near $124,000 in mid-2024. Cap rates have stabilized around 5.2%-5.7% for stabilized assets, and the construction pipeline is contracting sharply, which should support modest rent and occupancy gains by year-end 2026. Buyers are focused on well-located, newer suburban assets, while value-add and distressed deals are also clearing at significant discounts.
multifamily-dallas-tx.market_trend: Dallas multifamily sale prices per unit recovered from a mid-2024 trough of ~$124,000 to ~$168,000-$190,000 by Q4 2025 and Q1 2026, representing a roughly 35-50% rebound over the 24-month window, though distressed sales continue to clear at steep discounts to stabilized market pricing.
value_drivers: Pipeline contraction: DFW units under construction fell to a decade-low ~30,000 in Q3 2025, tightening future supply
Elevated vacancy at 12% citywide pressuring rents downward and pushing buyer cap rate requirements higher for older assets
Distressed deal flow: foreclosure auction sales clearing at $57,000-$82,000 per unit, dragging down blended market averages
Shift in transaction mix toward mid-rise and high-rise newer assets (35% of Q1 2026 deals vs. 18% a year prior), supporting price-per-unit appreciation for Class A product

multifamily-austin-tx.summary: Austin's multifamily market is working through a deep supply overhang following a 33% surge in apartment inventory from 2020 to 2025 -- the fastest rate nationwide -- which pushed vacancy to roughly 13.5% and rents down nearly 4.7% year-over-year as of Q1 2026. Transaction volume has picked up, rising 41.5% in 2025 versus 2024, though it still trails the decade average, with the average sale price per unit reaching approximately $177,000-$224,000 depending on asset class. Buyers are focusing on stabilized cash flow and well-located assets, with market cap rates settling near 5.7% in early 2026 as the supply pipeline begins to thin. The outlook is cautiously improving: absorption is outpacing deliveries, suggesting vacancy should continue to drift lower through 2026 and into 2027.
multifamily-austin-tx.market_trend: Multifamily sale prices in Austin declined from their 2022 peak and remained under pressure through 2024-2025 due to oversupply, but showed early stabilization in late 2025 with the average price per unit rising 2.1% to approximately $177,000 in 2025 and cap rates compressing modestly toward 5.5-5.7%.
value_drivers: Supply overhang: 30,002 new units delivered in 2025 (8.7% of stock) kept vacancy elevated at 13.5% in Q1 2026, pressuring rents and pricing
Thinning construction pipeline: new starts fell 18.9% year-over-year in 2025, reducing future supply pressure and supporting recovery expectations
Absorption outpacing deliveries: Austin absorbed 3,800 units vs. 2,000 delivered in Q1 2026, gradually burning through excess inventory
Distressed loan maturities driving deal flow: loan workouts and forced dispositions are pushing more assets to market at dislocated pricing, attracting value-add and opportunistic buyers

multifamily-charlotte-nc.summary: Charlotte's multifamily market is emerging from a historic supply surge, with over 16,000 units delivered in 2024 and another wave in 2025, pushing vacancy to around 8-12% and keeping rents slightly negative year-over-year. Despite elevated supply, demand has been exceptionally strong, absorbing a record 14,500 units in 2025, and the investment market is recovering with median per-unit prices rising about 19-28% from 2024 lows to roughly $211,000-$226,000 per unit. Cap rates have stabilized around 5.0%, and with construction starts now down roughly 40%, analysts expect improving fundamentals and a return to rent growth in 2026-2027. Institutional-grade trades are pricing in the $240,000-$300,000 per-unit range for newer, Class A product.
multifamily-charlotte-nc.market_trend: Charlotte multifamily sale prices trended lower through 2024, bottoming at a median of roughly $177,000-$184,000 per unit, then rebounded sharply in 2025 to approximately $211,000-$226,000 per unit as newer-vintage assets began trading, though prices remain below the 2021-2023 cycle peak.
value_drivers: Construction starts down approximately 40% from peak, signaling an approaching supply gap in 2026-2027 that should tighten vacancy and lift rents
Vacancy elevated at 8-12% due to 2024-2025 delivery wave, depressing NOI and keeping some sellers and buyers cautious
Record absorption of 14,500+ units in 2025 demonstrates exceptionally strong renter demand anchored by Charlotte's second-highest job growth nationally in 2025
Transaction mix shifting toward 2020s-vintage assets, with newer Class A product trading at $240,000-$300,000 per unit versus mid-$170,000s for older B/C stock in 2024

multifamily-las-vegas-nv.summary: The Las Vegas multifamily market has shown measured price appreciation over the past two years, with the median sale price rising from roughly $172,500 per unit in early 2024 to approximately $227,500 per unit by end of 2025, a gain of about 7%. Institutional buyers remain active, with several nine-figure trades closing in 2025, though elevated vacancy (near 9.4% in late 2025), new supply pressure, and elevated interest rates are keeping operators cautious. Cap rates have generally clustered in the low-to-mid 5% range for stabilized assets, with value-add and older Class C product trading at wider spreads. The market is expected to stabilize through 2026 as the construction pipeline slows and population-driven demand gradually absorbs recent deliveries.
multifamily-las-vegas-nv.market_trend: Las Vegas multifamily sale prices trended upward over the 24-month window, with the median price per unit rising from approximately $172,500 in the first half of 2024 to $227,500 by full-year 2025, driven by institutional demand for stabilized assets despite softer operating fundamentals.
value_drivers: Vacancy rose to ~9.4% in 2025 as heavy new supply deliveries outpaced absorption, pressuring asking rents and NOI
Multifamily construction deliveries fell ~30% in 2025 to roughly 4,000 units, beginning to relieve supply pressure through 2026
Median sale price per unit climbed 7% year-over-year in 2025, reflecting continued institutional demand for stabilized Class A and B assets
Elevated interest rates and stricter lending requirements are suppressing overall transaction volume and widening cap rates for value-add product
